captions.text: Should we be worried, Resident Evil, that you  went with "Requiem" as a subtitle? That is to say,
a memorial for something that has died? Then  again, perhaps it's overdue considering how
many times Resident Evil as a franchise has had  to resurrect itself. Which raises a question:
Resident Evil has worn so many faces over  the years; which one are we memorializing
here? The claustrophobic, unnerving survival  horror of its origins or the over-the-top
gonzo action horror of the Resident Evil 4  period? "Hey, why limit ourselves?" cries
Resident Evil Requiem. "Let's do both!" "Can  we also call back to the recent first-person,
more character-driven ones?" "The more the  merrier, Resident Evil 7 and 8! Come on in!"
"Don't forget to also turn into a big pile  of shit!" "Oh, piss off, Resident Evil 6!
Who keeps letting you in here?" "I'm a numbered  sequel, bitch! I'm still canon! I've got my own
parking space and everything!" So in a callback to  one of the earliest traditions of Resident Evil,
Requiem is split between two protagonists—one  male, one female—but instead of them both
basically playing the same except one gets a  lockpick and the other gets... biceps like he's
smuggling small, playful dogs in his arms, the  two here have vastly different gameplay styles.
On the one hand, we have Grace Ashcroft: a tiny,  nervous smol bean who doesn't have a s-s-single
line of d-d-dialogue where she pronounces the  first c-c-consonant less than three times and who
we play in first person, trying to sneak around  and hide from monsters as much as possible. Then
every now and again, we switch to third-person  mode to play as series mainstay Leon S. Kennedy
as he kicks the fucking door in and yells, "I  HEARD SOMEONE WAS TRYING TO BUILD ATMOSPHERE!"
before taking a fucking flamethrower to the  curtains and breaking out machine gun karate
on the zombie horde. The story opens with Grace,  an FBI agent, somehow—maybe she got in under the
useless twat recruitment drive—going into  a spooky abandoned building to investigate
mysterious deaths, zombie encounter, blah de  blah, fairly typical sort of introduction,
before she gets kidnapped by the inevitable smug,  mutated villain who, for today's performance,
will be played by Heath Ledger's Joker if he got  assimilated by the Borg. He takes her to a spooky
hospital, a unique setting that survival horror  has only explored 18 or 19,000 fucking times.
What matters is that the first section in the  hospital is some damned effective shit. You're
creeping around avoiding this reject Bloodborne  monster in the dark messing with lighting
mechanics, and you really feel Grace's full-blown  whimpering terror. The monster even invades the
room with the typewriter; that never fucking  happens. You can't do that! I called basies! And
then, at the end of this section, we flip channels  to Leon marching into the exact same hospital and
proceeding to get into a chainsaw duel with  a roomful of zombies in sexy doctor and nurse
outfits. And he hasn't missed a step since his  RE4 days, either in his kickass fighting skills
or in being terrible at one-liners. It might  sound like a lurching tonal shift, but honestly,
it works. Grace's gameplay is so unnerving that  it's a welcome relief to take a break and play as
an actually competent person for a bit to give  our knees a chance to re-solidify. I could've
gone for the game switching perspectives  more often, actually, 'cause after this,
we're stuck as Grace in the hospital for  a prolonged period, doing the usual thing:
exploring the map, finding the keys, solving the  incredibly contrived puzzles, and wasting a lot
of bullets on the space slightly above and to the  left of zombies' heads, or maybe that's just me.
Things never quite reach the heights of tension  they did at the beginning, though, and by the
time the hospital section was over, Leon's next  bit came like a freshly hydrated Pot Noodle to
a hungry man. He snipes! He roundhouse kicks! He  says bad one-liners! He meets the boss monsters
Grace has had to sneak around for the last three  hours and very cathartically beats them so hard
they'll have to stick Q-tips down their ears to  brush their teeth. Except I couldn't help glancing
behind the curtain and noticing there were an  awful lot of Pot Noodles stacked up. You see,
the second half of the game is mostly Leon.  And I noticed right after Requiem's release
a lot of social media posts to the effect,  "Boy, this game's really good so far!" from
accounts that all went mysteriously quiet after  enough time had passed that they could reach the
halfway point. Things start going downhill in  Leon's first section in the ruined city when I
started thinking, "Hang on, I'm feeling something.  Sort of like being very slowly run over by a car
loaded down with history books. What's the  word for that? Being alive during the second
Trump presidency? No, wait—boredom! That's  what it's called! I'm really fucking bored!"
Because the environments are all samey  ruined buildings full of generic zombies,
all the fear and tension is lost 'cause it's  in broad daylight, and our objective to gather
machine parts to complete a bomb feels very...  what's the word... workaday? Like we're playing
through Leon's commute? It's also when the  game starts awarding points for combat that
can be spent on weapon upgrades, and that doesn't  help because now it feels like we're helping Leon
fill out his tax return. But during this time,  the game keeps dropping random boss fights on us
that come out of nowhere with no explanation and  piss off just as fast. A giant spider appears;
you kill it. A giant plant monster appears; you  kill it. Fucking Mr. X from the Re2make appears,
as in literally drops through a ceiling  like the fucking Kool-Aid Man. "HEY,
KIDS! REMEMBER ME?" "Yeah, I remember it took a  lot longer to kill you last time, dickhead." "HEY,
WHAT DO YOU EXPECT FOR FIFTH BILLING?" And as  Leon closes in on a—you guessed it—secret Umbrella
underground research lab full of monsters in  apparently very shoddily made glass tubes designed
to fail the instant they catch the merest whiff  of protagonist energy, I get a sense the game has
turned into a Resident Evil: Greatest Hits album  curated by an easily distracted six-year-old.
There's another villain besides Borg  Joker who, when I first saw him,
I thought was Albert Wesker, and my little heart  skipped a beat 'cause that meant Resident Evil 5
was finally being jettisoned from continuity  to the delight of a grateful nation. But no,
he's an entirely unrelated sunglasses,  slicked-back hair, sharp suit-wearing, smug,
superpowered corporate prick. And as I was reeling  from that disappointment, the lab self-destructs
while we fight a giant wall of snot with various  conspicuous, glowing weak points. In other words,
the formula Resident Evil conclusion, and  not even a particularly wholehearted stab
at it. So I left the game feeling deeply let  down. Tried a New Game+ but found I couldn't
enjoy the good first half anymore now I knew what  it was leading to. It'd be like trying to warm
myself on the spark of a lit fuse connected to a  big bomb full of shit. This is such a consistent
pattern with Resident Evil games: they start  with a cool horror scenario or set piece that
works perfectly well without context but then  have this pathological need to explain it all
at the end in a secret lab of some kind. And the  explanation is inevitably nonsensical twaddle that
retroactively ruins all the lovely intrigue, and  I'd have been more satisfied if they'd just said,
"It was all done with magic; who cares?" You  know, like what Metal Gear Solid ended up doing.
